content:
# 1 Thessalonians Chapter 3 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF page 202 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1TH_003_page_001.jpg

---

**3:1** Therefore, no-longer enduring, we-thought-it-good to-be-left-behind in Athens alone,

**3:2** and we-sent Timothy, our brother and fellow-worker of-God in the good-news of-Christ, to-establish you and to-encourage [you] concerning your faith,

**3:3** [so] that no-one [would] be-shaken in these afflictions. For you yourselves know that for this we-are-appointed.

**3:4** For even when we-were with you, we-were-telling you beforehand that we-are-about to-suffer-affliction, just-as also it-happened and you-know.

**3:5** On-account-of this, I also, no-longer enduring, sent to-know your faith, lest somehow the-[one]-tempting tempted you and our labor might-become in vain.

**3:6** But now, Timothy having-come to us from you, and having-brought-good-news to-us [of] your faith and your love, and that you-have good remembrance of-us always, longing to-see us, just-as also we [long to see] you —

**3:7** on-account-of this we-were-encouraged, brothers, over you, upon all our distress and affliction, through your faith;

**3:8** because now we-live, if you stand-firm in [the] Lord.

**3:9** For what thanksgiving are-we-able to-return to-God concerning you, upon all the joy with-which we-rejoice on-account-of you before our God,

**3:10** night and day exceedingly-beyond praying to-see your face and to-complete the-[things]-lacking of-your faith?

**3:11** Now [may] our God and Father himself and our Lord Jesus direct our way toward you.

**3:12** And [may] the Lord make-you-to-increase and to-abound in love toward one-another and toward all, just-as also we [abound] toward you,

**3:13** to-establish your hearts blameless in holiness before our God and Father, at the coming of-our Lord Jesus with all his holy-ones. Amen.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **3:2 — "fellow-worker of God" (synergon tou theou)**: Codex Sinaiticus reads CΥΝΕΡΓΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ — "fellow-worker of God." This is the most striking variant in the chapter. Other manuscript traditions soften this bold phrase: some read "servant of God" (ΔΙΑΚΟΝΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ), others "servant of God and our fellow-worker" (ΔΙΑΚΟΝΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ ΚΑΙ CΥΝΕΡΓΟΝ ΗΜΩΝ), and Byzantine witnesses read simply "minister of God" (ΔΙΑΚΟΝΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ). The Sinaiticus reading, calling Timothy a "fellow-worker of God," is theologically the most daring and likely the most original, with later scribes evidently uncomfortable calling a human God's "co-worker." The EMET preserves the Sinaiticus reading.

2. **3:2 — "to establish" (sterixai)**: CΤΗΡΙΞΑΙ — an aorist infinitive meaning "to fix firmly, to set fast, to strengthen." The word carries the sense of making something immovable. Rendered "to-establish" to preserve the concrete, foundational nuance.

3. **3:3 — "be shaken" (sainesthai)**: CΑΙΝΕCΘΑΙ — a rare word in the NT (hapax legomenon), originally meaning "to wag the tail" (of a dog), hence "to fawn upon, to beguile, to disturb." In this context it means "to be moved, shaken, or unsettled." Some have suggested "beguiled" or "moved," but the context of afflictions favors "shaken."

4. **3:3 — "we are appointed" (keimetha)**: ΚΕΙΜΕΘΑ — literally "we are placed/set" for this purpose. The passive voice implies divine appointment to suffering, not mere coincidence. Preserved as "we-are-appointed."

5. **3:5 — Shift from "we" to "I"**: Paul shifts from the first person plural (ΜΗ ΚΕΤΙ CΤΕΓΟΝΤΕC, "no longer enduring" in 3:1) to the singular ΚΑΓΩ ("I also") in 3:5, suggesting that the decision to send Timothy was ultimately Paul's personal initiative, even if made in consultation with Silvanus. The EMET preserves this shift exactly.

6. **3:6 — "having-brought-good-news" (euangelisamenou)**: ΕΥΑΓΓΕΛΙCΑΜΕΝΟΥ — Paul uses the verb "to bring good news" (the same root as "gospel/good-news") to describe Timothy's report about the Thessalonians' faith. This is the only place in the NT where this verb is used for something other than proclaiming the Christian message. Preserved literally.

7. **3:10 — "exceedingly beyond" (hyperekperissou)**: ΥΠΕΡΕΚΠΕΡΙCCΟΥ — a compound adverb unique to Paul (also in 5:13 and Eph 3:20), built from three intensifying elements: ΥΠΕΡ (over/beyond) + ΕΚ (out of) + ΠΕΡΙCCΟΥ (abundance). Rendered with the hyphenated compound "exceedingly-beyond" to signal the stacked intensification in the Greek.

8. **3:11 — "direct" (kateuthynai)**: ΚΑΤΕΥΘΥΝΑΙ — aorist optative, expressing a wish or prayer. The singular verb with a compound subject ("God...and...Lord Jesus") is theologically significant, treating Father and Son as a unified agent. Preserved as singular "direct."

9. **3:13 — "holy ones" (hagion)**: ΑΓΙΩΝ — can mean "saints" (holy people) or "angels" (holy ones). The ambiguity is present in the Greek and is preserved here with "holy-ones" rather than importing a theological interpretation. The OT background (Zech 14:5; Deut 33:2) favors angelic beings, but Paul may intend both.

10. **3:13 — "Amen"**: Codex Sinaiticus includes ΑΜΗΝ at the end of verse 13. Some manuscripts omit it. The Sinaiticus reading is preserved.

-- EMET
